description: CaseFile Forensic Knowledge Base — curated DFIR references for grounded analysis

records.name: PowerShell Execution
records.description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution. PowerShell is a powerful interactive command-line interface and scripting environment included in Windows.

records.name: Windows Command Shell
records.description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows command shell (cmd.exe) for execution. The Windows command shell is the primary command prompt on Windows systems.

records.name: Windows Service (Persistence)
records.description: Adversaries may create or modify Windows services to repeatedly execute malicious payloads. When Windows boots up, it starts programs or applications called services that perform background system functions.

records.name: SMB/Windows Admin Shares
records.description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to interact with a remote network share using Server Message Block (SMB). The adversary may then perform actions as the logged-on user.

records.name: Valid Accounts
records.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining Initial Access, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, or Defense Evasion.

records.name: Match Legitimate Name or Location (Masquerading)
records.description: Adversaries may match or approximate the name or location of legitimate files or resources when naming/placing them. This is done for the sake of evading defenses and observation.
artifacts: MFT entries showing executables in temp/user directories

records.name: Clear Windows Event Logs
records.description: Adversaries may clear Windows Event Logs to hide the activity of an intrusion. Windows Event Logs are a record of a computer's alerts and notifications.

records.name: File Deletion
records.description: Adversaries may delete files left behind by the actions of their intrusion activity. Malware, tools, or other non-native files dropped or created on a system by an adversary may leave traces.

records.name: Scheduled Task
records.description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code.

records.name: LSASS Memory Credential Dumping
records.description: Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS).
artifacts: Event Log EID 4688 (Process Creation for mimikatz/procdump)

records.name: Windows Prefetch Analysis
records.description: Prefetch files (C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf) record application execution history including run count, last run times (up to 8), and referenced files/directories. Available on Windows client OS by default (disabled on Server unless registry-enabled).
records.forensic_value: Proves program execution even after the executable is deleted. Run count and timestamps establish frequency and timeline of activity.
caveats: Not available on Windows Server by default
Prefetch hash collision possible (different paths, same hash)
Last 8 run times only (Win10+), last 1 on Win7
File creation time ≈ first execution, modification time ≈ last execution

records.name: Amcache.hve Analysis
records.description: The Amcache registry hive (C:\Windows\appcompat\Programs\Amcache.hve) tracks application execution and installation metadata including SHA1 hashes, file paths, sizes, and timestamps.
records.forensic_value: Records SHA1 hash of executables at first execution — provides hash-based IOC matching even after file deletion. Links installer metadata to specific program installations.
caveats: Schema changed significantly between Windows versions
Not all executed programs appear
SHA1 hash may be for a different version than what ran
Timestamps may reflect compilation, not execution

records.name: Windows Event Log Analysis
records.description: Windows Event Logs (.evtx) record system, security, and application events. Security.evtx contains authentication, process creation, and privilege use events critical for incident response.
records.forensic_value: Authoritative source for authentication events, service installations, process creation (with command line if auditing enabled), and security policy changes.
caveats: Event logs can be cleared (EID 1102 records this)
Log rotation may lose older events
Process command line logging requires Advanced Audit Policy
Not all events are security-relevant

records.name: NTFS $MFT Analysis
records.description: The Master File Table ($MFT) is the core metadata structure of the NTFS file system. Every file and directory on an NTFS volume has at least one entry in the MFT containing timestamps, file sizes, and data locations.
records.forensic_value: Provides four sets of timestamps per file (Created, Modified, Accessed, Entry Modified for both $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME attributes). $FILE_NAME timestamps are harder to tamper with, enabling timestomping detection.
caveats: $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps can be modified by user-mode tools (timestomping)
$FILE_NAME timestamps are updated by the kernel and resist tampering
Comparing $SI vs $FN timestamps detects timestomping
Deleted file MFT entries may be overwritten

records.name: Windows Registry Analysis
records.description: The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database storing system configuration, user preferences, and application settings. Key hives for forensics: SAM, SECURITY, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, NTUSER.DAT, UsrClass.dat.
records.forensic_value: Records service configurations, startup programs, user activity (recent docs, typed URLs), USB device history, network connections, and installed software with timestamps.
caveats: Registry timestamps are per-key, not per-value
Deleted keys may be recoverable from unallocated hive space
Last write time updates when any value under the key changes
CurrentControlSet number determined from SYSTEM\Select\Current

records.name: Memory Forensics with Volatility 3
records.description: Memory forensics analyzes a snapshot of physical RAM to recover running processes, network connections, loaded modules, and other volatile artifacts not present on disk.
records.forensic_value: Provides ground truth about what was running at acquisition time. Can detect hidden/injected processes, recover encryption keys, and find malware that exists only in memory.

key_plugins.pslist: List processes from EPROCESS linked list
key_plugins.psscan: Scan for EPROCESS structures (finds hidden/unlinked processes)
key_plugins.netscan: List network connections and listening ports
key_plugins.cmdline: Show command line arguments for each process

caveats: pslist only shows linked processes — use psscan to find hidden ones
Memory acquisition changes system state slightly
Volatility truncates ImageFileName to 14 characters
Memory analysis requires correct OS profile/ISF symbols

records.name: Suspicious Service Installation
records.description: Detects installation of services with suspicious characteristics such as execution from temp directories, hex-named services, or services with unusual binary paths.
records.detection_logic: EID 7045 where ServiceName matches hex pattern OR ImagePath contains \temp\ OR ImagePath contains \perfmon\

records.name: Lateral Movement via Network Logon
records.description: Detects potential lateral movement through network logon events (Type 3) from internal IP addresses, especially when combined with explicit credential use (EID 4648).
records.detection_logic: EID 4624 LogonType=3 from internal RFC1918 source IP, correlated with EID 4648 explicit credential events from same source

records.name: Event Log Clearing Detected
records.description: Detects clearing of Windows Event Logs, a common anti-forensics technique to hide attacker activity.
records.detection_logic: EID 1102 (Security log cleared) OR EID 104 (System log cleared) OR Prefetch for wevtutil.exe with recent execution

records.name: Process Name Masquerading
records.description: Detects processes running with names of legitimate Windows binaries (csrss.exe, svchost.exe, lsass.exe) but from non-standard paths.
records.detection_logic: Process name matches known system binary BUT parent path is not \Windows\System32\ or expected location
false_positives: Software using same executable names legitimately (rare)

records.name: UTC Timeline Construction
records.description: A coherent UTC timeline correlates events across multiple artifact sources (event logs, prefetch, MFT, registry, memory) to establish the sequence of attacker actions. All timestamps must be normalized to UTC before correlation.
best_practices: Normalize all timestamps to UTC before comparison
Cross-reference at least 2 independent sources for key events
Account for timezone offsets in artifact-specific formats
Note clock skew between systems in multi-host investigations
Timeline events should be ordered chronologically with source attribution

records.name: Cross-Source Evidence Correlation
records.description: Correlating findings across multiple independent artifact sources increases confidence and detects inconsistencies. A process confirmed in both prefetch (execution evidence) and memory (running at acquisition time) is stronger than either alone.
best_practices: INFERRED = single source, consistent with other evidence
Flag contradictions between sources as separate findings
Process in memory + prefetch + amcache = highest confidence
Missing from one source is not contradiction — absence is not evidence of absence
